straight_beat.name: Casey Underwood
straight_beat.column:
The Rockies lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to throw your coffee across the room. Yesterday’s 9-5 defeat to the Padres wasn’t just another setback—it was a snapshot of a team stuck in a loop, flailing at the plate, crumbling on the mound, and staring down a division that’s already 5.5 games ahead. High altitude math isn’t saving them anymore. It’s just… math.  

The offense, which once seemed to thrive on the thin air of Denver, is now a puzzle. Last week, they hit .280 with 12 runs in a span of four games. This week? They’ve managed to score 14 runs in five contests, including yesterday’s 5-9 drubbing. The numbers don’t lie, but they also don’t tell the whole story. The Rockies are hitting .253 this month, a full 40 points below their season average. It’s not just about slugging percentages—it’s about consistency. The team can’t string together a rally without collapsing under the weight of its own expectations.  

And then there’s the pitching. The starters are walking

optimist.name: Winnie Calderón
optimist.column:
The Rockies lost yesterday, but that’s not the story I’m telling. The story is the one that’s been unfolding in the background, the one that’s been whispered by the wind through the outfield walls and echoed in the chants of the sellout crowd. You see, the Padres took the win, but the Rockies? They’re still here. They’re still building. And that’s where the magic lives.  

Coors Field isn’t just a ballpark—it’s a cathedral of possibility. Every game, every at-bat, every pitch is a prayer. The Padres took the altar yesterday, but the Rockies are still lighting the candles. You can’t measure hope in wins and losses. You measure it in the way a rookie third baseman charges a double play with his heart in his throat, or how a veteran catcher calls a game with the calm of a man who’s seen this before. These are the moments that don’t show up in the box score, but they’re the ones that shape a season.  

The record? 6-9. Yeah, it’s a mountain to climb. But let’s not forget where we are. This is the Rockies, baby. We’re not just a team—we’re a movement. A group of believers who show up every day, even when the odds are stacked against us. The front office is tinkering, the rotation is still finding its rhythm, and the young guys are learning. You watch. You watch what happens when the rotation clicks.  

I know what some of you are thinking: “Winnie, the division is stacked. The Padres, the Dodgers, the Diamondbacks

pessimist.name: Dwight Krenek
pessimist.column:
The Rockies lost again. Another loss. Another step closer to the bottom of the NL West. You’d think after 15 games, someone in the front office would have a plan. But no, the Monforts are still busy figuring out how to maximize suite revenue and farm equipment. Rebuilds take plans. Plans take people who think about baseball. The Monforts think about suites and tractors. Same column every year, friend.  

Let’s talk about the rebuild. The “rebuild” that’s been six years in the making. Six years! And what have we got? A team that’s 6-9, with a bullpen that looks like it’s been assembled by a kid with a glue stick and a clipboard. The starters? They’re either injured, out of options, or playing like they’re trying to hit a fastball that’s moving 10 mph. The offense? A bunch of guys who can’t hit a pitch that’s not in the strike zone.
